french: VERS Aquitaine par insuls Britanniques,
De par eux mesmes grandes incursions :
Pluyes,gelées feront terroirs iniques,
Port Selyn fortes fera invasions.
english: Toward Aquitaine by the British Isles,
From themselves great incursions:
Rains, frosts will make the lands iniquitous,
Port Selin will make strong invasions.
1.interpretation: British forces will launch major raids into Aquitaine from their islands. Extreme weather—persistent rains and severe frosts—will devastate the countryside, rendering it barren and lawless, while the port of Selin (often interpreted as Salonika or a symbolic eastern harbour) becomes the staging point for powerful invasions.

french: La teste blue fera la teste blanche
Autant de mal que France a faict leur bien :
Mort a l’anthene, grand pendu sus la branche,
Quand prins des siens le Roy dira combien.
english: The blue head will make the white head,
As much evil as France has done them good:
Death to the antenna, great hanged upon the branch,
When taken by his own the King will say how much.
2.interpretation: A shift from blue (perhaps royalist or revolutionary) to white (Bourbon or opposite faction) occurs, bringing as much harm as France previously brought benefit. A prominent figure is executed or hanged; when the king learns the betrayal came from his own circle, he will demand to know the full cost or extent of the treachery.

french: Pour la chaleur solaire sus la mer
De Negrepont les poissons demy cuits,
Les habitans les viendront entamer,
Quand Rhod & Gennes leur faudra le biscuit.
english: Due to the solar heat upon the sea
Of Negroponte the fish half-cooked,
The inhabitants will come to eat them,
When Rhodes and Genoa will lack the biscuit.
3.interpretation: Extreme solar heat overheats the waters around Negroponte (Euboea), partially cooking the fish. Starving locals will eat them raw or half-cooked out of necessity, while the islands of Rhodes and Genoa face severe food shortages (lacking even hardtack 'biscuit')—depicting famine amid climatic catastrophe in the eastern Mediterranean.

french: Depuis Monach jusqu'aupres de Sicille,
Toute la plage demourra desolée,
Il n'y aura fauxbourgs, cité, ne ville,
Que par Barbares pillée soit & vollée.
english: From Monaco as far as near Sicily,
All the coast will remain desolate,
There will be no suburbs, city nor town,
That will not be pillaged and robbed by Barbarians.
4.interpretation: The entire western Mediterranean coastline from Monaco to Sicily will be left devastated and empty. No settlement—suburb, city, or town—will escape plunder and looting by barbarian (likely North African or Ottoman) forces, foretelling a sweeping coastal raid or conquest.

french: Qu’en dans poisson, fer & lettre enfermée,
Hors sortira, qui puis fera la guerre,
Aura par mer sa classe bien ramée,
Apparoissant pres de Latine terre.
english: When inside fish, iron and letter enclosed,
Will come out the one who then will make war,
He will have by sea his fleet well-oared,
Appearing near the Latin land.
5.interpretation: A secret message sealed inside a fish (or a fish-shaped container) containing iron (perhaps a key or weapon) will be retrieved, unleashing a war leader. He will command a powerful oared fleet that appears off the Italian ('Latin') coast—evoking a surprise naval invasion from a hidden source.

french: Aupres des portes & dedans deux citez
Seront deux fleaux & onc n’apperceu un tel,
Faim, dedans peste, de fer hors gens boutez,
Crier secours au grand Dieu immortel.
english: Near the gates and inside two cities
There will be two scourges never before seen,
Famine within, plague; people driven out by iron,
Cry for help to the great immortal God.
6.interpretation: Two neighbouring or twin cities will suffer unprecedented twin calamities: famine and plague inside the walls, while armed forces ('iron') drive the population out through the gates. The desperate survivors will call upon divine aid amid the horror.

french: Entre plusieurs aux isles deportez,
L’un estre nay à deux dents en la gorge :
Mourront de faim les arbres esbrotez,
Pour eux neuf Roy, nouvel edict leur forge.
english: Among many deported to the islands,
One being born with two teeth in the throat:
The uprooted trees will die of hunger,
For them a new king will forge a new edict.
7.interpretation: Among exiles banished to islands, one is born with an ominous sign—two teeth already in the throat (a prodigy of violence or precocity). Vegetation withers from neglect or drought; a new ruler issues a severe decree specifically for these deportees, likely tightening their confinement.

french: Temples sacrés prime façon Romaine,
Rejetteront les goffres fondements,
Prenant leurs lois premieres & humaines,
Chassant, non tout, des saincts les cultements.
english: Sacred temples of the first Roman fashion
Will reject the hollow foundations,
Taking their first and human laws,
Driving out, not all, the cults of the saints.
8.interpretation: Ancient-style Roman temples will be restored, rejecting later corrupt or hollow additions. They will revive original civil and humanistic Roman law while partially suppressing saint veneration—indicating a classical revival that reforms but does not fully abolish Christian practices.

french: Neuf ans le regne le maigre en paix tiendra,
Puis il cherra en soif si sanguinaire,
Pour luy grand peuple sans foy & loy mourra,
Tué par un beaucoup plus de debonnaire.
english: For nine years the lean one will hold the reign in peace,
Then he will fall into such a bloodthirsty thirst,
For him the great people without faith and law will die,
Killed by one far more debonair.
9.interpretation: A thin, austere ruler maintains peace for nine years before succumbing to savage aggression. The populace, lacking faith and legal protection, perishes under his tyranny, only to be ultimately destroyed by a seemingly kinder but more deadly successor.

french: Avant long temps le tout sera rangé,
Nous esperons un siecle bien senestre :
L’estat des masques & des seuls bien changé,
Peu trouveront qu’a son rang vueille estre.
english: Before long everything will be arranged,
We hope for a truly sinister century:
The state of masks and of the solitary well changed,
Few will find that they wish to be at their rank.
10.interpretation: A major reordering of society is imminent. The coming era is hoped for but turns out sinister. The status of hypocrites ('masks') and isolated individuals is radically altered; most people will reject their assigned place in the new hierarchy.

french: Le prochain fils de l’aisnier parviendra,
Tant eslevé jusqu'au regne des fors :
Son aspre gloire un chacun la craindra,
Mais ses enfants du regne gettez hors.
english: The next son of the elder will attain,
So highly elevated to the rule of the strong:
His harsh glory everyone will fear,
But his children thrown out of the reign.
11.interpretation: The younger son of an elder ruler rises to command powerful forces. His ruthless reputation terrifies all, yet his own heirs are expelled from power—indicating a brief, fearsome reign followed by dynastic failure.

french: Yeux clos, ouverts d’antique fantasie,
L’habit des seuls seront mis à neant :
Le grand monarque chastiera leur frenaisie,
Ravir des temples le tresor par devant.
english: Eyes closed, opened by ancient fantasy,
The garb of the solitary will be reduced to nothing:
The great monarch will chastise their frenzy,
Ravishing the treasure from temples beforehand.
12.interpretation: A ruler, inspired by ancient visions, strips the poor or monks ('solitary') of their clothing and punishes their excesses. He seizes temple treasures in advance—depicting an authoritarian who loots religious institutions while enforcing austerity.

french: Le corps sans ame plus n’estre en sacrifice,
Jour de la mort mis en natiVité :
L’esprit divin fera l’ame felice,
Voyant le verbe en son esternité.
english: The body without soul no longer to be in sacrifice,
Day of death set in nativity:
The divine spirit will make the soul happy,
Seeing the Word in its eternity.
13.interpretation: Human sacrifice of soulless bodies ends. Death becomes intertwined with birth (perhaps a new spiritual cycle). The divine spirit brings eternal joy to the soul by beholding the divine Word—signalling a profound eschatological or redemptive shift.

french: A Tours, Gien, gardé seront yeux penetrants,
Descrouvriront de loing la grand sereine :
Elle & sa suite au port seront entrans,
Combat, poussez, puissance souveraine.
english: At Tours, Gien, vigilant eyes will be kept,
They will discover from afar the great serene one:
She and her suite will enter the port,
Combat, pushed, sovereign power.
14.interpretation: Watchmen in Tours and Gien spot a majestic fleet or lady ('great serene') approaching from afar. She and her retinue land at the harbour, triggering battle and the assertion of supreme authority—suggesting a powerful female-led invasion or arrival.

french: Un peu devant monarque trucidé :
Castor, Pollux en nef, astre crinite :
L’erain public par terre & mer vuidé,
Pise, Ast, Ferrare, Turin, terre interdicte.
english: A little before a monarch is slain:
Castor, Pollux in a ship, hairy star:
The public bronze emptied by land and sea,
Pisa, Asti, Ferrara, Turin, land forbidden.
15.interpretation: Shortly before a king is murdered, the Gemini twins (Castor & Pollux) appear as a celestial sign in a comet-tailed ship. Public bronze (coins, bells, or cannon) is stripped from land and sea, and four northern Italian cities become prohibited zones—foretelling regicide amid economic collapse and military occupation.

french: Naples, Palerme, Sicille, Syracuses,
Nouveaux tyrans, fulgures feux celestes :
Force de Londres, Gand, Bruxelles, & Suses,
Grand hecatombe, triomphe faire festes.
english: Naples, Palermo, Sicily, Syracuse,
New tyrants, lightning celestial fires:
Force from London, Ghent, Brussels, and Susa,
Great hecatomb, triumph making feasts.
16.interpretation: Southern Italian and Sicilian cities fall to new tyrants amid lightning and heavenly fire (bombs or comets). Northern European powers (London, Ghent, Brussels) and Susa (Persia) join forces, leading to massive slaughter followed by victorious celebrations.

french: Le camp du temple de la vierge vestale,
Non esloigné d'Ethene & monts Pyrenées :
Le grand conduict est caché dans la male,
North getez fleuves et vignes mastinées.
english: The camp of the temple of the Vestal Virgin,
Not far from Etna and the Pyrenees mountains:
The great conduit is hidden in the male,
North thrown rivers and vines masticated.
17.interpretation: A military encampment is established near a temple of the Vestal Virgin, located not far from Mount Etna and the Pyrenees. A major aqueduct or secret channel is concealed within a 'male'. Northern forces cast rivers into chaos, and vines are chewed or destroyed—suggesting a northern invasion disrupting southern European lands, water supply, and agriculture.

french: Nouvelle et pluye subite, impetueuse,
Empeschera subit deux exercites :
Pierre, ciel, feux faire la mer pierreuse,
La mort de sept terre & marin subites.
english: Sudden and impetuous new rain,
Will suddenly hinder two armies:
Stone, sky, fires will make the sea stony,
Sudden death of seven on land and sea.
18.interpretation: A torrential downpour abruptly stops two opposing armies. A cataclysm of stone (hail or landslide), sky (storm), and fire turns the sea into a rocky hazard, causing the sudden death of seven key figures or units on both land and water.

french: Nouveaux venus lieu basty sans defense,
Occuper la place par lors inhabitable :
Pres, maisons, champs, villes prendre à plaisance,
Faim, peste, guerre, arpen long labourable.
english: New arrivals, a place built without defence,
Will occupy the place then uninhabitable:
Preys, houses, fields, towns taken at pleasure,
Famine, plague, war, long arable labour.
19.interpretation: Invaders seize undefended settlements, rendering them unlivable. They plunder homes, farms, and towns at will, ushering in famine, disease, and prolonged conflict that turns fertile land into a battlefield requiring endless toil.

french: Freres & seurs en divers lieux captifs
Se trouveront passer pres du monarque :
Les contempler ses rameaux ententifs,
Desplaisant voir menton, front, nez, les marques.
english: Brothers and sisters captive in various places
Will be found passing near the monarch:
To contemplate his branches attentive,
Displeasing to see chin, forehead, nose, the marks.
20.interpretation: Imprisoned siblings from different locations are brought before the ruler. Observing his followers ('branches') reveals disturbing facial marks—perhaps scars, brands, or deformities—indicating torture or a cursed lineage.

french: L’ambassadeur envoyé par biremes,
A my chemin d’incogneus repoulsez :
De sel renfort viendront quatre triremes,
Cordes & chaines en Negrepont troussez.
english: The ambassador sent by biremes,
Halfway on an unknown path repulsed:
From salt four triremes will come as reinforcement,
Ropes and chains torn in Negroponte.
21.interpretation: An envoy dispatched on twin-oared ships is turned back midway. Four triremes arrive from the sea ('salt') as reinforcements, breaking the harbour chains at Negroponte—depicting a failed diplomatic mission followed by naval intervention.

french: Le camp Ascop d'Europe partira,
S’adioignant proche de l'Isle submergée :
D’Arton classe phalange pliera,
Nombril du monde plus grand voix subrogée.
english: The Ascop camp of Europe will depart,
Joining near the submerged island:
The Arton fleet phalanx will bend,
The navel of the world will have a greater substituted voice.
22.interpretation: A European military camp relocates near a sunken island. The Arton naval-phalanx formation yields; the central world power ('navel') is supplanted by a stronger new authority—signalling a major geopolitical shift.

french: Palais, oyseaux, par oyseau dechassé,
Bien tost apres le Prince parvenu :
Combien qu’hors fleuve ennemy repoulsé,
Dehors saisi trait d’oyseau soustenu.
english: Palaces, birds, chased by a bird,
Very soon after the Prince arrived:
However much the enemy was repulsed beyond the river,
Outside seized the bird’s sustained line.
23.interpretation: Royal residences and symbolic birds are driven out by a rival force ('bird'). Shortly after the prince's arrival, despite pushing the enemy across a river, a key position or banner ('bird’s line') is captured—indicating a reversal despite initial success.

french: Bestes farouches de faim fleuves tranner,
Plus part du champ encontre Hister sera,
En caige de fer le grand fera treisner,
Quand rien enfant de Germain observera.
english: Fierce beasts of hunger will cross rivers,
Most of the field will be against Hister:
In an iron cage the great one will drag,
When the child of Germain observes nothing.
24.interpretation: Starving wild animals (or desperate soldiers) ford rivers. The main battle occurs against the Hister (Danube). A powerful figure is imprisoned in iron and dragged away while a young German ('Germain') watches passively—foretelling a major defeat near the Danube.

french: La garde estrange trahira forteresse,
Espoir & umbre de plus hault mariage :
Garde deceue, fort prinse dans la presse,
Loire Saone, Rosne, Gar à mort outrage.
english: The foreign guard will betray the fortress,
Hope and shadow of a higher marriage:
Guard deceived, the fort taken in the press,
Loire, Saône, Rhône, Garonne to death outrage.
25.interpretation: Mercenary guards betray a stronghold. A promised high marriage brings false hope. The deception leads to the fort's capture; the major French rivers (Loire, Saône, Rhône, Garonne) witness deadly outrage—suggesting widespread betrayal and violence.

french: Pour la faveur que la cité fera,
Au grand qui tost perdra camp de bataille,
Fuis le rang Pau Thesin versera,
De sang, feux mors, noyez de coup de taille.
english: For the favour that the city will show
To the great one who will soon lose the field of battle,
Flee the line of the Po, the Ticino will pour forth
Blood, fire, deaths, drowned by a blow of the sword.
26.interpretation: A city extends favour or alliance to a commander who rapidly loses the battle. The defeated forces flee along the Po River line; the Ticino River overflows with blood, fire, corpses, and drowning victims from merciless sword strikes—depicting a disastrous rout in northern Italy with rivers turning into scenes of slaughter.

french: Le divin verbe sera du ciel frappé,
Qui ne pourra proceder plus avant :
Du resrerant le secret estoupé,
Qu’on marchera par dessus & devant.
english: The divine word will be struck from the sky,
Which will no longer be able to proceed further:
The secret stopper of the reserved one is removed,
That one will march over and beyond.
27.interpretation: A divine revelation or sacred pronouncement is suddenly interrupted from heaven, preventing its further progress. The seal or restraint on the hidden secret is broken, allowing humanity to trample over it and advance—suggesting a dramatic breach of sacred boundaries, enabling new doctrines or secular paths to emerge.

french: Le penultiesme du surnom du prophete,
Prendra Diane pour son jour & repos :
Loin vaguera par frenetique teste,
Et delivrant un grand peuple d'impos.
english: The penultimate of the prophet’s surname
Will take Diana for his day and rest:
Far will he wander with frantic head,
And delivering a great people from imposition.
28.interpretation: A leader whose name or title derives from the second-to-last part of a prophet’s designation will adopt Diana (symbol of the moon, rest, or feminine power) as his emblem or day of observance. He will wander restlessly in madness, yet ultimately liberate a large oppressed population from heavy burdens or taxes—portraying an erratic yet redemptive revolutionary or messianic figure.

french: L'oriental sortira de son siege,
Passer les monts Apennins voir la Gaule :
Transpercera le ciel, les eaux & neige,
Et un chacun frappera de sa gaule.
english: The Oriental will leave his seat,
Cross the Apennine mountains, see Gaul:
He will pierce the sky, the waters and snow,
And each one will strike with his own pole.
29.interpretation: An eastern ruler departs his throne, crosses the Apennines, and invades Gaul (France). He unleashes attacks from above (perhaps missiles, artillery, or celestial phenomena) through rain and snow, while every soldier strikes with his personal staff or weapon—foretelling a major eastern-led winter campaign into western Europe.

french: Un qui les dieux d'Annibal infernaux,
Fera renaistre, effrayeur des humains :
Oncq' plus d'horreur ne plus dire journaulx,
Qu'avint viendra par Babel aux Romains.
english: One who the infernal gods of Hannibal
Will make reborn, terror of mankind:
Never more horrors nor worse daily news
Than what will come to the Romans through Babel.
30.interpretation: A figure revives the dark, hellish legacy of Hannibal, becoming a universal terror. Unprecedented horrors and grim reports reach the Romans (or modern Italy/West) via 'Babel' (confusion, a multilingual coalition, or symbolic eastern power)—predicting a terrifying resurgence of ancient enmity against Rome.

french: En Campanie le Cassilin fera tant,
Qu'on ne verra que d'aux les champs couvers :
Devant apres la pluye de longtemps,
Hors mis les arbres rien l'on verra de verts.
english: In Campania Cassilin will be so much
That only fields covered with waters will be seen:
Before and after the long rain,
Except for the trees nothing green will be seen.
31.interpretation: The area around Cassino in Campania suffers extreme flooding, submerging all fields. After prolonged heavy rain, the landscape remains waterlogged and lifeless except for trees—nothing green survives—depicting a devastating, enduring inundation.

french: Laict, sang, grenouilles escoudre en Dalmatie,
Conflict donné, peste près de Balennes,
Cry sera grand par toute Esclavonie,
Lors naistra monstre près & dedans Ravenne.
english: Milk, blood, frogs will rise in Dalmatia,
Conflict given, plague near Balenna:
Great cry throughout all Slavonia,
Then a monster will be born near and inside Ravenna.
32.interpretation: In Dalmatia, unnatural phenomena (milk, blood, frogs emerging) appear amid war and plague near Balenna. Widespread mourning echoes across Slavonia, followed by the birth of a monstrous figure or portent near and within Ravenna—evoking apocalyptic omens and a monstrous emergence in Italy.

french: Par le torrent qui descend de Veronne,
Par lors qu'au Pau guidera son entrée :
Un grand naufrage, & non moins en Garonne,
Quand ceux de Gennes marcheront leur contrée.
english: By the torrent that descends from Verona
When it will force its entry into the Po,
A great shipwreck, and no less in the Garonne,
When those of Genoa will march their country.
33.interpretation: Floodwaters from Verona surge into the Po River, enabling large vessels to navigate inland and suffer shipwreck. A similar catastrophe strikes the Garonne when Genoese forces march through their territory—foretelling catastrophic flooding and military exploitation of swollen rivers.

french: L'ire insensée du combat furieux,
Fera à table par freres le fer luire :
Les departir, blessé, curieux,
Le fier duelle viendra en France nuire.
english: The senseless wrath of furious combat
Will make the iron shine at table by brothers,
They will divide the dead, wounded, curious:
The proud duel will come to harm France.
34.interpretation: Blind fury in savage battle causes brothers to display bloodied weapons at the table. They divide the casualties (dead, wounded, onlookers); a vainglorious duel ultimately damages France—depicting fratricidal strife and a destructive personal challenge.

french: Dans deux logis de nuict le feu prendra,
Plusieurs dedans ostouffez & rostis :
Pres de deux fleuves pour seul il adviendra :
Sol l'Arq, & Caper tous seront amortis.
english: In two dwellings at night fire will take hold,
Several inside stifled and roasted.
Near two rivers for sure it will happen,
Sun, the Arch, and Caper all will be extinguished.
35.interpretation: Two buildings burn at night, suffocating and incinerating those trapped inside. The fire occurs near two rivers, and key symbols or sites (Sun, Arch, Caper) are put out—foretelling a deadly conflagration in a strategic riverine location.

french: Du grand Prophete les lettres seront prinses,
Entre les mains du tyran deviendront,
Frauder son Roy seront ses entreprinses,
Mais ses rapines bien tost le troubleront.
english: The letters of the great Prophet will be taken
Into the hands of the tyrant they will become:
To defraud his king will be his enterprises,
But his robberies will soon trouble him.
36.interpretation: Sacred or prophetic writings are seized by a tyrant who uses them to deceive his sovereign. His schemes involve cheating the king, but his own plunder will quickly bring him distress—indicating a tyrant whose theft of holy texts leads to his downfall.

french: De ce grand nombre que l'on envoyera,
Pour secourir dans le fort assiegez,
Peste & famine tous les devorera,
Hors mis septante qui seront profligez.
english: From this great number that will be sent
To relieve the besieged in the fort,
Plague and famine will devour them all,
Except seventy who will be wasted.
37.interpretation: A large relief force sent to aid a besieged fortress is instead consumed by plague and starvation. Only seventy survive, but they are squandered or destroyed—depicting a tragic failed rescue where disease and hunger prove deadlier than the enemy.

french: Des condamnez sera fait un grand nombre,
Quand les Monarques seront conciliez :
Mais l'un d'eux viendra si malencombre,
Que guere ensemble ne seront raliez.
english: A great number will be made of the condemned
When the monarchs will be reconciled:
But to one of them such misfortune will come
That wars together will not be reunited.
38.interpretation: Mass executions occur during a period of royal reconciliation. Yet one monarch suffers such disaster that joint military campaigns cannot be restored—foretelling a fragile peace shattered by calamity.

french: Un'an devant le conflit Italique,
Germains, Gaulois, Espagnols pour le fort,
Cherra l'escolle maison de république,
Ou, hors mis peu, seront suffoquez mors.
english: One year before the Italian conflict,
Germans, Gauls, Spaniards for the fort:
The republican house of school will fall,
Where, except few, will be suffocated dead.
39.interpretation: One year prior to a major war in Italy, German, French, and Spanish forces converge on a fortified republican institution (likely a school or assembly hall symbolizing republican ideals). Almost everyone inside is asphyxiated—depicting a pre-war massacre targeting a symbolic center of republicanism.

french: Un peu apres non point longue intervalle,
Par mer & terre sera faict grand tumulte.
Beaucoup plus grande sera pugne navalle,
Feux, animaux, qui plus feront d'insulte.
english: A little after, not a long interval.
By sea and land great tumult will be made.
Much greater will be the naval battle,
Fires, animals, that will add more insult.
40.interpretation: Shortly after a brief pause, massive chaos erupts on land and sea, with the naval engagement proving far larger. Fires and beasts (cavalry or war animals) intensify the outrage—foretelling an all-out war dominated by sea power.

french: La grand estoille par sept jours bruslera,
Nuée fera deux soleils apparoir,
Le gros mastin toute nuict hurlera,
Quand grand pontife changera de terroir.
english: The great star will blaze for seven days,
A cloud will make two suns appear:
The big mastiff will howl all night
When the great pontiff changes his land.
41.interpretation: A brilliant star shines intensely for a week, creating a double-sun illusion through clouds. A powerful 'mastiff' (leader or nation) howls in distress during the night, coinciding with the pope relocating his seat—portending a dramatic astronomical sign tied to papal upheaval.

french: Coq, chiens, & chats de sang seront repeus,
Et de la playe du tyran trouvé mort,
Au lict d'un autre jambes & bras rompus,
Qui n'avoit peu mourir de cruel mort.
english: Rooster, dogs, and cats will be satiated with blood,
And from the wound of the tyrant found dead,
In the bed of another legs and arms broken,
Who had no fear of dying a cruel death.
42.interpretation: Symbolic beasts (French rooster, dogs, cats) feast on blood amid the discovery of a tyrant’s fatal wound. Another figure lies broken in bed, unafraid of a brutal end—depicting violent retribution and fearless martyrdom.

french: Durant l'estoille chevelue apparente,
Les trois grands princes seront faits ennemis :
Frappez du ciel paix terre tremulente,
Pau, Timbre undans, serpent sus le bort mis.
english: During the appearance of the hairy star,
The three great princes will be made enemies:
Strikes from the sky, trembling peace on earth.
Po, beating Tiber, serpent placed on the raised border.
43.interpretation: A comet ('hairy star') heralds the turning of three major rulers against one another. Heavenly blows disrupt earthly peace; the Po and Tiber rivers surge while a serpent (threat or army) is positioned on the frontier—foretelling war sparked by celestial omen.

french: L'aigle poussée entour des pavillons,
Par autres oyseaux d'entour sera chassée :
Quand bruit des cymbres tube & sonnaillons
Rendront le sens de la dame insensée.
english: The eagle pushed into the tower of the palisades
Will be chased by other birds around,
When the noise of cymbals, tubes and bells
Will restore sense to the insane lady.
44.interpretation: An eagle (imperial power) is driven into a fortified tower and pursued by rival forces ('birds'). A cacophony of instruments revives the reason of a mad woman (perhaps a nation or figure in delirium)—suggesting chaos followed by sudden clarity or recovery.

french: Trop le ciel pleure l'Androgyn procrée,
Pres de ciel sang humain respandu :
Par mort trop tard grand peuple recrée,
Tard & tost vient le secours attendu.
english: Too much the sky weeps for the androgyne progeny,
Near this sky human blood spilled,
By death too late the great people recreated,
Late and soon comes the awaited help.
45.interpretation: Heaven mourns an androgynous lineage or hybrid creation; human blood flows nearby. Death arrives too late to prevent rebirth of a great people, and long-awaited aid finally arrives—depicting tragic loss followed by renewal and deliverance.

french: Après grand troche humain plus grand s'appreste
Le grand moteur les siècles renouvelle :
Pluye, sang, laict, famine, fer & peste,
Au ciel veu feu, courant longue estincelle.
english: After great human trouble, a greater one prepares,
The great mover renews the centuries.
Rain, blood, milk, famine, iron, and plague
Seen in the sky, a long-running fire sparkles.
46.interpretation: Following immense human suffering, an even larger calamity looms, driven by a cosmic force that restarts historical cycles. Celestial signs of rain, blood, milk, famine, weapons, and plague appear with a prolonged fiery streak—foretelling apocalyptic renewal.

french: L'ennemy grand vieil dueil meurt de poison,
Les souverains par infiniz subjuguez :
Pierres plouvoir, cachez soubs la toison.
Par mort articles en vain sont alleguez.
english: The great old enemy dies of poison:
The sovereigns are infinitely subjugated.
Stones rain down, hidden under the fleece:
By death articles in vain are alleged.
47.interpretation: An ancient adversary succumbs to poison; rulers are permanently subdued. Hidden treasures ('stones') under wool (perhaps a deceptive cover) are revealed, but death renders claims futile—suggesting poisoned victory and buried wealth exposed in vain.

french: La grand copie que passera les monts,
Saturne en l'Arcq tournant du poisson Mars :
Venins cachez soubs testes de saulmons,
Leur chief pendu à fil de polemars.
english: The great copy that will cross the mountains.
Saturn in Sagittarius turning the fish Mars
Poisons hidden under the heads of salmons:
Their chief hung on the pole-mars thread.
48.interpretation: A major document or force crosses the mountains under a Saturn-in-Sagittarius alignment affecting Mars (in Pisces). Concealed poisons lurk beneath salmon heads (perhaps deceptive supplies); the leader is executed by hanging on a 'pole-mars' (perhaps a spear or pole)—foretelling astrological-timed treachery and execution.

french: Les conseillers du premier monopole,
Les conquerants seduits par la Melite :
Rodes, Bisance pour leurs exposants pole,
Terre faudra les poursuivans de fuite.
english: The advisers of the first monopoly,
The conquerors seduced for Melite:
Rhodes, Byzantium for their exposing pole:
The land will need the pursuers of flight.
49.interpretation: Counsellors of an original dominant power seduce conquerors with the lure of 'Melite' (Malta or a prize). Rhodes and Byzantium reveal a central stake; the earth demands pursuit of those who flee—depicting intrigue over a strategic Mediterranean asset.

french: Quand ceux d'Hainault, de Gand & de Bruxelles
Verront à Langres le siege devant mis,
Derrier leurs flancs seront guerres cruelles,
La playe antique sera pis qu'ennemis.
english: When those of Hainault, of Ghent and of Brussels
See the siege set before Langres,
Behind their flanks there will be cruel wars,
The ancient wound will be worse than enemies.
50.interpretation: People from the Low Countries (Hainaut, Ghent, Brussels) observe a siege at Langres (in eastern France). Fierce conflicts arise in their rear (possibly in their homeland), and an old grievance or unresolved injury proves more damaging than any external enemy—likely pointing to internal division or betrayal amplifying external threats.

french: Le sang du juste à Londres fera faute,
Bruslez par foudres de vingt trois les six,
La dame antique cherra de place haute,
De mesme secte plusieurs feront occis.
english: The blood of the just in London will be faulted / demanded,
Burned by thunderbolts of twenty-three sixes,
The ancient lady will fall from a high place,
Several of the same sect will be killed.
51.interpretation: Often associated with events in London: the unjust execution of a righteous figure (e.g., Charles I, 'blood of the just' shed), the Great Fire of 1666 (burning 'thunderbolts' perhaps evoking flames or the date 1666 as 'twenty threes the sixes'—a common but debated numerological link), the fall of an 'ancient lady' (possibly the old St. Paul's Cathedral or a symbolic figure like the monarchy/church), and persecution of a religious/political faction (Puritans or Royalists). The quatrain remains cryptic and multi-applicable.

french: Dans plusieurs nuits la terre tremblera,,
Sur le printemps deux efforts suite,
Corinthe, Ephese aux deux mers nagera,
Guerre s’esmeut par deux vaillants de luite.
english: During several nights the earth will tremble,
In the spring, two efforts in succession,
Corinth and Ephesus will swim in two seas,
War stirred up by two valiant ones in combat.
52.interpretation: Multiple nights of earthquakes. In spring, two successive attempts or assaults occur. Corinth and Ephesus (ancient cities on different seas: Corinthian Gulf and Aegean) are metaphorically flooded or involved in naval strife. War is ignited by two brave leaders or champions—suggesting seismic activity followed by conflict in the eastern Mediterranean or Greece/Turkey region.

french: La grande peste de cité maritime,
Ne cessera que mort ne soit vengee
Du juste sang par pris damné sans crime,
De la grand dame par feinte n'outragee.
english: The great plague of the maritime city
Will not cease until the death is avenged
Of the just blood, condemned for a price without crime,
Of the great lady outraged by pretense.
53.interpretation: A severe plague strikes a coastal city and persists until vengeance for an innocent's unjustly condemned death (perhaps a martyr or victim sold out). A prominent woman ('great lady') is wronged through deception but not overturned—implying prolonged suffering until justice for the innocent restores balance.

french: Par gent estrange, & Romains loingtaine,
Leur grand cité apres eaue fort troublee,
Fille sans trop different domaine,
Prins chef, ferreure n'avoir esté riblee.
english: By foreign people and distant Romans,
Their great city after water greatly troubled,
Daughter without too different domain,
Taken chief, fettered, not having been railed at.
54.interpretation: Foreigners and distant 'Romans' (perhaps Italian or imperial forces) disrupt a major city with severe flooding or turmoil ('water'). A 'daughter' (city, colony, or symbolic figure) in a similar realm is captured; its leader taken prisoner without reproach—pointing to invasion, flood damage, and conquest with little resistance.

french: Dans le conflit le grand qui peu valloit,
A son dernier fera cas merveilleux,
Pendant qu'Hadrie verra ce qu'il falloit,
Dans le banquet pongnale l'orgueilleux.
english: In the conflict the great one who was worth little,
At his last will do a marvelous deed,
While the Adriatic will see what was necessary,
In the banquet stabs the proud one.
55.interpretation: A seemingly insignificant leader performs an astonishing act in battle at his end. The Adriatic region witnesses what was required, and during a feast or banquet, the arrogant figure is stabbed—suggesting treachery or assassination amid conflict in the Adriatic/Italian area.

french: Que peste & glaive n'a sçeu definer,
Mort dans le puys sommet du ciel frappé :
L'Abbé mourra quand verra ruiner,
Ceux du nauffrage l'escueil voulant grapper.
english: What plague and sword could not finish,
Death in the well, summit of the sky struck:
The abbot will die when he sees ruined
Those from the shipwreck wanting to grasp the reef.
56.interpretation: A calamity beyond plague or war strikes a high place ('summit struck'). An abbot dies witnessing destruction; shipwreck victims desperately cling to rocks—evoking a disaster (perhaps avalanche, lightning, or meteor) in mountains combined with maritime peril and famine.

french: Avant conflict le grand tombera :
Le grand à mort, mort, trop subite & plainte,
Nay mi parfaict, la plus part nagera,
Aupres du fleuve de sang la terre tainte.
english: Before the conflict the great one will fall:
The great to death, death too sudden and lamented,
Born imperfect, most will swim,
Near the river the earth stained with blood.
57.interpretation: Prior to battle, a major structure or leader collapses suddenly, causing widespread grief. Many drown ('swim' in blood/flood); land near a river turns red with blood—foretelling a pre-war disaster like a dam break or bridge failure leading to massive casualties.

french: Sans pied ne main par dent ayguë & forte,
Par globe au fort de port & lainé nay,
Pres du portail desloyal transporte,
Silene luit, petit, grand emmené.
english: Without foot or hand, by sharp and strong tooth,
By globe to the strong one born of wool at the port,
Near the disloyal portal transported,
The moon shines, small and great led away.
58.interpretation: A limbless or disabled figure attacks fiercely ('tooth'). A spherical object or cannon reaches a fortified wool-born (perhaps sheep/ram symbol or port) entity. Something is covertly moved near a treacherous gate under moonlight; both humble and mighty are taken—hinting at stealthy warfare or ambush.

french: Classe Gauloise par appuy de grand garde,
Du grand Neptune, & ses tridens souldars,
Rongee Provence pour soustenir grand bande,
Plus Mars Narbon par javelots & dards.
english: The French fleet by support of great guard,
Of great Neptune and his trident soldiers,
Gnawed Provence to sustain the great band,
More Mars at Narbonne by javelins and darts.
59.interpretation: A French naval force, aided by strong protectors and sea powers (Neptune's trident-armed troops), ravages or is ravaged in Provence to support a large army. War (Mars) intensifies at Narbonne with thrown weapons—indicating naval-land operations in southern France.

french: La foy Punique en Orient rompue,
Grand Iud, & Rosne, Loyre, & Tag, changeront
Quand du mulet la faim sera repue,
Classe espargie, sang et corps nageront.
english: Punic faith broken in the Orient,
Great Judah, Rhône, Loire, and Tagus will change,
When the mule's hunger is satisfied,
Fleet scattered, blood and bodies will swim.
60.interpretation: Carthaginian-like (treacherous) trust collapses in the East. Major rivers/regions (perhaps symbolic powers) undergo transformation. Once a stubborn 'mule' (laborer or hybrid force) is appeased, ships disperse amid slaughter—suggesting betrayal in eastern alliances leading to naval bloodshed.

french: Euge, Tamins, Gironde & la Rochelle,
O sang Troyen Mort au port de la flesche,
Derrier le fleuve au fort mise l'eschelle,
Pointes feu grand meurtre sus la bresche.
english: Euge, Thames, Gironde and La Rochelle,
O Trojan blood! Death at the port of the arrow,
Behind the river the ladder placed at the fort,
Points of fire, great murder upon the breach.
61.interpretation: Areas including the Thames, Gironde, and La Rochelle face war linked to ancient Trojan lineage (perhaps French/British conflict). Death strikes at a burning or arrow-marked port; siege ladders rise behind a river, with incendiary weapons causing slaughter at a breached wall—depicting a fiery riverine assault.

french: Mabus puis tost alors mourra, viendra,
De gens & bestes une horrible defaite,
Puis tout à coup la vengeance on verra,
Cent, main, soif, faim, quand courra la comete.
english: Mabus then soon will die, there will come
A horrible rout of people and beasts:
Then suddenly vengeance will be seen,
Hundred, hand, thirst, hunger, when the comet runs.
62.interpretation: A figure or code-named 'Mabus' dies abruptly, triggering massive slaughter of humans and animals. Swift retribution follows, bringing widespread famine, thirst, and chaos under a comet's appearance—often seen as heralding a major destructive leader or event with apocalyptic aftermath.

french: Gaulois, Asone bien peu subiuguera,
Pan, Marne & Seine fera Perme l'urie,
Qui le grand mur contre eux dressera,
Du moindre au mur le grand perdra la vie.
english: Gauls Ausonia will subdue very little,
Po, Marne, and Seine will make the urine permeate,
He who raises the great wall against them,
From the least to the wall the great one will lose his life.
63.interpretation: French forces barely conquer Italy (Ausonia). Rivers (Po, Marne, Seine) overflow disastrously ('permeate'). A leader building a massive barrier against invaders dies, dooming his cause—indicating failed defenses amid flooding and limited conquest.

french: Seicher de faim, de soif, gent Genevoise,
Espoir prochain viendra au deffaillir,
Sur point tremblant sera loy Gebenoise,
Classe au grand port ne se peut acueillir.
english: Dried up by hunger and thirst, Genevan people,
Nearby hope will come to fail,
On a trembling point the Genevan law will be,
Fleet at the great port cannot be received.
64.interpretation: Geneva's inhabitants face starvation and dehydration. Imminent relief collapses; the city's laws waver amid instability. Its harbor rejects incoming ships—foretelling siege, blockade, or crisis causing famine and legal/societal breakdown.

french: Le parc enclin grande calamité,
Par l'Hesperie & Insubre fera,
Le feu en nef, peste & captivité,
Mercure en l'Arc Saturne fenera.
english: The inclined park great calamity,
By Hesperia and Insubria will do,
Fire on ship, plague and captivity,
Mercury in the Arc, Saturn will wither.
65.interpretation: A vulnerable region ('inclined park') suffers disaster from western (Hesperia/Spain or Italy) and Lombard (Insubria) forces. Ships burn, spreading disease and enslavement. Astrological Mercury in Sagittarius ('Arc') under Saturn brings decay—suggesting astrological-timed naval/plague catastrophe in Italy/Spain.

french: Par grands dangiers le captif eschapé,
Peu de temps grand a fortune changee :
Dans le palais le peuple est attrapé,
Par bon augure la cité assiegee.
english: Through great dangers the captive escaped,
In little time great fortune changed:
In the palace the people are trapped,
By good omen the city besieged.
66.interpretation: A prisoner escapes peril and swiftly rises in status. Yet the masses remain confined in the palace; a seemingly positive sign ironically precedes a siege—warning of false hope before encirclement and hardship.

french: Le blonds au nez forche viendra commettre,
Par le duelle & chassera dehors,
Les exilez dedans fera remettre,
Aux lieux marins commettant les plus forts.
english: The blond with forked nose will come to commit,
By duel and drive out,
The exiles inside will put back,
To marine places committing the strongest.
67.interpretation: A fair-haired, cruel ('forked nose') leader engages in combat/duels to expel foes. Exiles are forcibly returned to coastal areas, where the mightiest are drafted or committed—indicating violent leadership enforcing repatriation and naval/military conscription.

french: De l'Aquilon les effors seront grands,
Sus l'Occean sera la porte ouverte :
Le regne en l'Isle sera reintegrand,
Tremblera Londres par voille descouverte.
english: From the North the efforts will be great,
Upon the Ocean the gate will be opened:
The kingdom on the Isle will be reintegrated,
London will tremble by sail uncovered.
68.interpretation: Powerful northern forces ('Aquilon') open a sea passage. An island realm is reabsorbed or restored; London quakes at the sight of an approaching fleet ('uncovered sail')—predicting a major naval invasion or reclamation from the north.

french: Le Roy Gaulois par la Celtique dextre,
Voyant discorde de la grand Monarchie,
Sus les trois parts fera florir son sceptre,
Contre la cappe de la grand Hierarchie.
english: The Gaulish King by the Celtic right hand,
Seeing discord in the great Monarchy,
Over the three parts will make his scepter flourish,
Against the cape of the great Hierarchy.
69.interpretation: The French king, supported by Celtic allies, exploits divisions in a vast empire. He extends power across three domains, opposing the 'cape' (hat/authority) of a supreme hierarchy (likely papal or imperial)—signaling French expansion challenging dominant religious/political order.

french: Le dard du ciel fera son estendue,
Morts en parlant, grande execution,
La pierre en l'arbre la fiere gent rendue,
Bruit humain monstre purge expiation.
english: The dart from the sky will make its extension,
Dead speaking, great execution,
The stone in the tree the proud people rendered,
Human noise, monster purge expiation.
70.interpretation: A heavenly 'dart' (comet, lightning, or projectile) extends destruction, causing mass death ('dead speaking' perhaps echoes or omens). A stone in a tree symbolizes submission of the arrogant; a monstrous human figure is purged for atonement—evoking celestial judgment and cleansing of tyranny.

french: Les exilez en Sicile viendront,
Pour delivrer de faim la gent estrange,
Au point du jour les Celtes luy faudront
La vie demeure à raison Roy se range.
english: The exiles will come to Sicily,
To deliver the foreign people from hunger,
At daybreak the Celts will fail him,
Life remains by reason: the King aligns himself.
71.interpretation: Exiles arrive in Sicily to relieve starving foreigners. At dawn, Celtic allies desert or fail; the king pragmatically adjusts his stance for survival—suggesting a relief effort in Sicily that shifts alliances and royal policy.

french: Armee Celtique en Italie vexee,
De toutes parts conflict & grande perte,
Romains fuis, ô Gaule repoulsee,
Pres du Thesin, Rubicon pugne incerte.
english: Celtic army in Italy vexed,
Conflict and great loss on all sides,
Romans fled, O Gaul repulsed,
Near the Ticino, Rubicon uncertain battle.
72.interpretation: French/Celtic troops suffer in Italy amid widespread fighting and casualties. Romans retreat, but Gaul is pushed back; combat near the Ticino and Rubicon rivers remains undecided—depicting inconclusive warfare in northern Italy.

french: Au lac Fucin de Benac le rivage,
Prins du Leman au port de l'Orguion,
Nay de trois bras predict bellique image,
Par trois couronnes au grand Endymion.
english: At Lake Fucino from Benacus the shore,
Taken from Léman at the port of Orguion,
Born of three arms predicts warlike image,
By three crowns to the great Endymion.
73.interpretation: Events link Lake Fucino (drained in antiquity) and Lake Garda (Benacus), with a leader from Lake Geneva (Léman) at Orguion port. A three-branched symbol heralds war; three crowns bestowed on 'Endymion' (mythic sleeper/king figure)—suggesting a tripartite alliance or coronation preparing conflict in northern Italy.

french: De Sens, d'Autun viendront jusque au Rosne,
Pour passer outre vers les monts Pyrenees,
La gent sortir de la marque d'Anconne,
Par terre & mer suivra à grand traînees.
english: From Sens, from Autun they will come as far as the Rhône,
To pass beyond toward the Pyrenees mountains,
The people leave the March of Ancona,
By land and sea they will follow with great trails.
74.interpretation: Forces from Sens and Autun advance to the Rhône, heading toward the Pyrenees. Groups depart the Ancona march (Italian border), pursuing over land and sea in large numbers—indicating mass movement or migration/invasion toward the western mountains.

french: La voix ouye de l'insolit oyseau,
Sur le canon du respiral estage
Si haut viendra du froment le boisseau,
Que l'homme d'homme sera Anthropophage.
english: The voice heard of the unusual bird,
On the cannon of the breathing floor,
So high will come the measure of wheat,
That man will become cannibal of man.
75.interpretation: An strange bird's call (omen) sounds atop a 'breathing cannon' (perhaps chimney or vent). Wheat prices soar extremely, driving humanity to cannibalism—portraying apocalyptic famine where desperation overrides morality.

french: Foudre en Bourgongne fera cas portenteux,
Que par engin oncques ne pourroit faire,
De leur senat sacriste faict boiteux.
Fera sçavoir aux ennemis l'affaire.
english: Lightning in Burgundy will cause a portentous event,
That no engine could ever achieve,
Of their sacrilegious senate made lame,
Will reveal the affair to the enemies.
76.interpretation: A lightning strike in Burgundy will trigger an extraordinary event impossible by human means. A corrupt or sacrilegious senate will be crippled, thereby exposing their schemes to adversaries—suggesting divine intervention or a natural disaster that unmasks political betrayal.

french: Par arcs feux, poix & par feux repoussez,
Cris hurlements sur la minuit ouys :
Dedans sont mis par les rempars cassez,
Par canicules les traditeurs fuys,
english: By fire-arrows, pitch and by fires repulsed,
Cries and howls heard at midnight:
Inside they are placed by the broken ramparts,
Through the dog-days the traitors flee.
77.interpretation: Burning arrows and pitch are used to repel attackers, causing screams and howls at midnight. Enemies penetrate through shattered defenses, while traitors escape via underground passages during the hottest summer period—depicting a night assault on a besieged city with internal betrayal.

french: Le grand Neptune du profond de la mer,
De gent Punique et sang Gaulois meslé :
Les Isles à fang pour le tardif ramer,
Plus luy nuira que l'occult mal celé,
english: The great Neptune from the depth of the sea,
Of Punic people and Gaulish blood mixed:
The islands to blood for the late rowing,
Will harm him more than the hidden celestial evil.
78.interpretation: A powerful naval force (symbolized as Neptune), composed of mixed Carthaginian (Punic) and French (Gaulish) elements, will bring bloodshed to islands through delayed naval efforts. This mixed fleet will suffer more harm than from any concealed heavenly threat—indicating a costly naval campaign in the Mediterranean.

french: La barbe crespe & noire par engin,
Subjuguera la gent cruelle & fiere :
Le grand CHIREN ostera du lungin,
Tous les captifs par Seline banière.
english: The curly and black beard by engine,
Will subjugate the cruel and proud people:
The great CHIREN will remove from the long place,
All the captives by the Seline banner.
79.interpretation: A leader with a curly black beard, using cunning or machines ('engin'), will conquer fierce and arrogant peoples. The great CHIREN (an anagram often linked to Henry or a future ruler) will liberate captives held under the lunar ('Seline') banner—suggesting a military campaign that frees prisoners from an eastern or Islamic force.

french: Après conflict du lesé l'eloquence,
Par peu de temps se trame faint repos :
Point l'on n'admet les grands à delivrance,
Des ennemis sont remis à propos.
english: After the conflict the eloquence of the injured,
In a short time a false peace is plotted:
The great ones are not admitted to deliverance,
The enemies are put back on the agenda.
80.interpretation: Following a war, the eloquent pleas of the wounded lead to a brief, insincere truce. Powerful figures are denied release, and former enemies are brought back into negotiations—pointing to fragile post-conflict diplomacy and unresolved grievances.

french: Par feu du ciel la cité presque aduste,
L'urne menace encor Deucalion,
Vexees Sardaigne par la Punique fuste,
Après que Libra lairra son Phaëton.
english: By fire from the sky the city is almost burned,
The urn still threatens Deucalion,
Sardinia vexed by the Punic ship,
After Libra leaves its Phaethon.
81.interpretation: A celestial fire nearly destroys a city. The 'urn' (possibly Aquarius or a vessel) continues to threaten a flood (Deucalion myth). Sardinia is harassed by a North African (Punic) vessel, following an astrological shift in Libra involving Phaethon (a comet or bright star)—combining heavenly signs, fire, and naval threats.

french: Par faim la proye fera loup prisonnier,
L'assaillant lors en extresme détresse,
Le nay ayant au devant le dernier,
Le grand n'eschappe au milieu de la presse,
english: By hunger the prey will make the wolf prisoner,
The attacker then in extreme distress,
The born one having the last in front,
The great one does not escape in the middle of the crowd.
82.interpretation: Famine forces the hunted to trap the hunter ('wolf'). The aggressor falls into dire straits, and a leader (the 'great one') cannot escape amid the throng—depicting a reversal where starvation turns the tables on invaders or predators during conflict.

french: Le gros traffic d'un grand Lyon changé,
La plus part tourne en pristine ruine,
Proye aux soldats par pille vendange :
Par Jura mont & Sueve bruine.
english: The great commerce of a great Lyon changed,
The greater part turns to pristine ruin,
Prey to the soldiers by plundered harvest:
By Jura mountain and Suevian drizzle.
83.interpretation: Lyon’s thriving trade collapses into desolation. Most of it reverts to ruin, with soldiers seizing plundered harvests. The Jura mountains and Suevian (Germanic) regions experience cold rain—indicating economic breakdown, military pillage, and supply shortages in eastern France and beyond.

french: Entre Campaigne, Sienne, Flora, Tustie,
Six mois neuf jours ne pleuvera une goutte :
L'estrange langue en terre Dalmatie,
Courira sus, vastant la terre toute.
english: Between Campania, Siena, Florence, Tuscany,
Six months and nine days not a drop will rain:
The foreign tongue in Dalmatian land
Will run over it, wasting the whole land.
84.interpretation: A prolonged drought (six months and nine days) strikes central Italy (Campania to Tuscany). A foreign language invades and devastates Dalmatia, spreading ruin across the region—combining extreme weather with conquest or cultural imposition.

french: Le vieux plain barbe soubs le statut severe,
A Lyon faict dessus l'Aigle Celtique,
Le petit grand trop outre persevrere,
Bruit d'arme au ciel, mer ronge Lygustique.
english: The old plain-bearded under the severe statute,
In Lyon does above the Celtic Eagle:
The small great too far will persevere,
Noise of arms to the sky: the Ligurian sea reddened.
85.interpretation: An elderly, simply bearded leader under strict rule acts in Lyon under the symbol of the Celtic Eagle. A modest but ambitious force pushes too far, while the clamor of battle reaches the heavens and the Ligurian Sea turns red with blood—suggesting militarism in Lyon leading to a bloody naval conflict off southern France.

french: Naufrage à classe près d'onde Hadriatique,
La terre tremble esmuë sur l'air en terre mis,
Egypte tremble augment Mahometique,
L'Herault soy rendre à crier est commis.
english: Shipwreck to the fleet near the Adriatic wave,
The earth trembles, stirred, air mixed with earth,
Egypt trembles, Mahometan increase,
The herald commits himself to crying out.
86.interpretation: A naval disaster strikes a fleet in the Adriatic. An earthquake mixes air and earth (possibly a tsunami or dust storm). Egypt shakes as Islamic ('Mahometan') power grows, and a herald is forced to proclaim the news loudly—linking maritime loss, seismic events, and rising influence in the eastern Mediterranean.

french: Après viendra des estremes contrées,
Prince Germain, desssus le throsne doré :
La servitude et eaux rencontrées,
La dame serve, son temps plus n'adoré.
english: After will come from extreme countries,
German Prince upon the golden throne:
Servitude and waters met,
The servant lady, her time no longer adored.
87.interpretation: From distant lands a German prince ascends a golden throne. Oppression and floods coincide, while a once-revered female servant or figure loses her status—hinting at a shift in power toward Germanic rule amid social upheaval and natural disasters.

french: Le circuit du grand faict ruineux,
Le nom septiesme du cinquiesme sera :
D'un tiers plus grand l'estrange belliqueux,
Mouton, Lutece, Aix ne garentira.
english: The cycle of the great ruinous deed,
The seventh name of the fifth will be:
Of a third larger the foreign belligerent,
Montauban, Lutetia, Aix will not protect.
88.interpretation: A destructive cycle reaches its seventh iteration from the fifth. A foreign enemy, one-third stronger, emerges, and key cities (Montauban, Paris/Lutetia, Aix) fail to provide safety—foretelling escalating warfare that overwhelms familiar defenses.

french: Un jour seront demis les deux grands maîstres,
Leur grand pouvoir se verra augmenté :
La terre neuve sera en ses hauts estres,
Au sanguinaire le nombre raconté.
english: One day the two great masters will be halved,
Their great power will be seen increased,
The new land will be in its high estates,
To the bloodthirsty the number is told.
89.interpretation: Two powerful rulers or entities are divided, yet their individual strength paradoxically grows. A newly formed territory rises in prominence, while a warlike figure counts his forces—suggesting a partition that strengthens both sides or a new power emerging from division.

french: Par vie & mort changé règne d'Ongrie,
La loy sera plus âspre que service :
Leur grand cité d'urlemens plaincts et crie,
Castor et Pollux ennemis dans la lice.
english: By life and death the reign of Hungary changed,
The law will be harsher than servitude:
Their great city full of howls, laments and cries,
Castor and Pollux enemies in the lists.
90.interpretation: Hungary’s kingdom transforms through life and death (succession or revolution). Laws become more oppressive than slavery, causing the capital to echo with cries. Legendary twins Castor and Pollux become rivals in combat—symbolizing bitter internal conflict or civil strife within the realm.

french: Soleil levant un grand feu lon verra,
Bruit & clarté vers Aquilon tendant.
Dedans le rond mort & cris lon orra,
Par glaive feu, faim, mort les attendans.
english: At sunrise a great fire will be seen,
Noise and brightness tending toward the north wind.
Inside the circle death and cries will be heard,
By sword, fire, hunger, death awaits them.
91.interpretation: At dawn a massive fire or explosion appears, with noise and light moving northward. Within a circular area, screams and death occur, accompanied by sword, fire, famine, and death—evoking a catastrophic event (possibly volcanic, explosive, or military) in a northern direction.

french: Feu couleur d'or du ciel en terre veu,
Frappé du haut nay, faict cas merveilleux :
Grand meurtre humain : prinse du grand neveu,
Morts d'espectacles eschappé l'orgueilleux.
english: Golden-colored fire from the sky seen on earth,
Struck from on high, a marvelous event:
Great human slaughter: capture of the great nephew,
Deaths in the spectacle, the proud one escaped.
92.interpretation: A golden meteor or fireball falls to earth, creating a spectacular incident. Massive killing follows; a prominent nephew is captured, many perish in the chaos, but an arrogant figure escapes—suggesting a celestial disaster leading to violence where elites evade justice.

french: Bien près du Tymbre presse la Lybitine,
Un peu devant grand inondation :
Le chef du nef prins, mis à la sentine,
Chasteau, palais en conflagration.
english: Very near the Tiber the Libyan presses,
A little before a great inundation:
The captain of the ship taken, put in the bilge,
Castle and palace in conflagration.
93.interpretation: Near the Tiber River (Rome), Libyan or North African forces advance. Shortly before a major flood, the ship’s captain is captured and imprisoned below deck, while castles and palaces burn—combining invasion near Rome with flooding and fire.

french: Grand Pau, grand mal pour Gaulois recevra.
Vaine terreur au maritin Lyon :
Peuple infiny par la mer passera,
Sans eschapper un quart d'un million.
english: Great Po, great evil for the Gauls will receive,
Vain terror at the maritime Lyon:
Infinite people will pass by the sea,
Without a quarter of a million escaping.
94.interpretation: The great River Po will bring terrible suffering to the French (Gauls). Useless panic grips the port city of Lyon. Vast numbers of people attempt to flee by sea, but a quarter-million fail to escape—foretelling a catastrophic flood or invasion along the Po with massive loss of life.

french: Les lieux peuplez seront inhabitables,
Pour champs avoir grande division :
Règnes livrez à prudents incapables,
Lors les grands frères mort & dissention.
english: The populated places will be uninhabitable,
For fields to have great division:
Kingdoms delivered to prudent incapables:
Then the great brothers death and dissension.
95.interpretation: Inhabited areas become unlivable due to severe division of agricultural lands. Wise but ineffective leaders receive control of realms, leading to the death of powerful brother-figures and resulting discord—indicating environmental or territorial collapse followed by political failure and strife.

french: Flambeau ardant au ciel soir sera veu,
Pres de la fin & principe du Rosne,
Famine, glaive, tard le secours pourveu,
La Perse tourne envahir Macédoine.
english: A burning torch in the evening sky will be seen,
Near the end and the source of the Rhône,
Famine, sword: late the aid provided,
Persia turns to invade Macedonia.
96.interpretation: A fiery comet or meteor appears at dusk near the Rhône River’s source or end. Famine and war delay relief efforts. Persia (or a Middle Eastern power) invades Macedonia—linking a celestial sign with delayed help and renewed eastern conflict.

french: Romain Pontife garde de t'approcher.
De la cité que deux fleuves arrouse,
Ton sang viendra auprès de la cracher,
Toy & les tiens quand fleurira la rose.
english: Roman Pontiff, beware of approaching
The city watered by two rivers,
Your blood will come near to spitting,
You and yours when the rose will bloom.
97.interpretation: The Pope is warned against nearing a city at the confluence of two rivers. Bloodshed will occur nearby, affecting the Pope and his followers when a 'rose' (symbol of a dynasty, England, or a flower) flourishes—suggesting danger to the papacy in a strategic river city during a time of symbolic blooming.

french: Celuy du sang reperse le visage,
De la victime proche sacrifice,
Tenant en Leo, augure par presage,
Mais estre à mort lors pour la fiancée.
english: He of the blood will sprinkle the face
Of the nearby sacrificial victim:
Holding in Leo, omen by presage,
But to be put to death then for the betrothed.
98.interpretation: A blood-relative will perform or witness the sprinkling of a sacrificial victim. An astrological position in Leo serves as an omen, yet someone is condemned to death on account of a fiancée or bride—pointing to a ritualistic or political killing tied to marriage and celestial signs.

french: Terroir Romain qu'interpretoit augure,
Par gent Gauloise par trop sera vexée :
Mais nation Celtique craindra l'heure,
Boreas, classe trop loing l'avoit poussée.
english: Roman territory that interpreted the omen,
Will be too much vexed by the Gaulish people:
But the Celtic nation will fear the hour,
Boreas had pushed the fleet too far.
99.interpretation: A Roman region skilled in omens suffers greatly from French (Gaulish) actions. The Celtic nation dreads the critical moment, as the north wind (Boreas) drives a fleet excessively far—suggesting strained relations between Rome and France alongside a naval mishap caused by winds.

french: Dedans les isles si horrible tumulte,
Rien on n'orra qu'une bellique brigue,
Tant grand sera des predateurs l'insulte,
Qu'on se viendra ranger à la grand ligue.
english: Within the islands such horrible tumult,
Nothing will be heard but a warlike brig,
So great will be the insult of the predators,
That they will come to align with the great league.
100.interpretation: The islands experience terrible disorder, filled only with the sounds of warlike vessels. Pirate or raider aggression becomes so severe that it forces alignment with a major alliance—predicting chaotic island conflicts that ultimately lead to a large naval coalition against marauders.
